1: King of Babylon, famous for promulgating one of the earliest sets of written laws in history: the Code of Hammurabi.
2: Egyptian pharaoh considered the most powerful of all time. He reigned for 66 years and built Abu Simbel.
3: Ancient Greek poet, author of the Iliad and the Odyssey, the two great epics of Western literature.
4: Chinese philosopher whose teachings profoundly influenced the culture of East Asia for more than two millennia.
5: Spiritual teacher of ancient India, founder of Buddhism, one of the world's great religions.
6: Greek philosopher considered one of the founders of Western philosophy. Teacher of Plato.
7: Greek philosopher, student of Socrates and teacher of Aristotle. He founded the Academy of Athens.
8: Greek philosopher, student of Plato and teacher of Alexander the Great. Father of formal logic.
9: King of Macedonia who built one of the largest empires in ancient history, conquering from Greece to India.
10: Greek mathematician known as the father of geometry. His work Elements was the principal mathematical text for centuries.
11: Greek mathematician, physicist, and engineer. One of the greatest scientists of antiquity.
12: Carthaginian general, one of the finest military strategists in history. He crossed the Alps with elephants during the Second Punic War.
13: Roman politician, orator, and philosopher. One of the greatest writers in Latin and a defender of the Roman Republic.
14: Roman politician and general, one of the most influential figures in history. Dictator of Rome until his assassination on the Ides of March.
15: Last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt. Ally and lover of Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.
16: First Roman emperor. He transformed the Roman Republic into the Roman Empire during his long reign.
17: Roman epic poet, author of the Aeneid, the Georgics, and the Eclogues. Considered the greatest Latin poet.
18: Roman poet, author of the Metamorphoses, a masterwork of Latin literature. Exiled by Augustus.
19: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher. His Meditations are a masterpiece of personal philosophy.
20: King of the Franks and first Holy Roman Emperor. He unified much of Western Europe.
21: Persian physician and philosopher. His Canon of Medicine served as a reference text in Europe for centuries.
22: Founder and first Great Khan of the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous empire in history.
23: Medieval philosopher and theologian, central figure of scholasticism. Canonized by the Catholic Church.
24: Venetian merchant and explorer. His journey to China and the court of Kublai Khan opened Europe to the East.
25: Italian poet, author of the Divine Comedy. Considered the father of the Italian language.
26: French heroine and saint of the Catholic Church. She led the French army during the Hundred Years' War.
27: German inventor of the movable-type printing press. His invention revolutionized the transmission of knowledge.
28: Navigator and explorer who reached America in 1492, initiating the era of European exploration.
29: Italian Renaissance genius: painter, sculptor, architect, musician, mathematician, engineer, inventor, and scientist.
30: Italian philosopher and diplomat. His work The Prince defined modern political science.
31: Astronomer who formulated the heliocentric model of the solar system, initiating the Scientific Revolution.
32: Painter, sculptor, architect, and Renaissance poet. Author of the Sistine Chapel ceiling and the David.
33: Italian Renaissance painter and architect, one of the three great masters of the High Renaissance.
34: Portuguese explorer who organized the first expedition to circumnavigate the Earth.
35: German monk who initiated the Protestant Reformation, one of the most important events in the Western world.
36: Italian astronomer, physicist, and mathematician. Considered the father of observational astronomy and modern physics.
37: English playwright and poet, considered the most important writer in the English language.
38: Spanish writer, author of Don Quixote, considered the first modern novel.
39: Dutch painter and etcher. Considered one of the great masters of European art.
40: French philosopher, mathematician, and scientist. Father of modern philosophy and creator of analytic geometry.
41: English physicist and mathematician. He formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation, and invented calculus.
42: German Baroque composer and musician, considered one of the greatest composers in history.
43: German Baroque composer, naturalized British. Author of the oratorio Messiah.
44: French writer, historian, and philosopher. Central figure of the Enlightenment and advocate for freedom of expression.
45: Swiss philosopher whose political and educational ideas profoundly influenced the French Revolution.
46: Central German philosopher of the Enlightenment. His Critique of Pure Reason revolutionized modern philosophy.
47: Austrian composer and pianist of the Classical period. He composed over 600 works and is considered a musical genius.
48: German composer and pianist, central figure in the transition between Classicism and Romanticism.
49: French general and statesman who dominated Europe during the Napoleonic Wars. Emperor of the French.
50: British general and statesman. He defeated Napoleon at the Battle of Waterloo and served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom.
51: American polymath: scientist, inventor, politician, diplomat, and one of the Founding Fathers of the United States.
52: Scottish philosopher and economist. His work The Wealth of Nations is considered the foundation of modern economics.
53: 16th president of the United States. He led the country during the Civil War and abolished slavery.
54: English naturalist and biologist. He developed the theory of evolution by natural selection.
55: German philosopher, economist, and political theorist. His ideas about capitalism and socialism transformed the world.
56: Victorian English novelist, one of the most important writers of the 19th century. Author of Oliver Twist.
57: French chemist and microbiologist. Father of microbiology, he developed pasteurization and the rabies vaccine.
58: Russian novelist, author of War and Peace and Anna Karenina. One of the most important figures in world literature.
59: Russian novelist, author of Crime and Punishment, The Idiot, and The Brothers Karamazov.
60: Polish Romantic composer and pianist. Considered one of the greatest composers for piano.
61: Hungarian composer and pianist. Considered the most virtuosic pianist of the 19th century.
62: German composer, primarily of operas. His work influenced the history of Western music.
63: French writer, author of Les Misérables and The Hunchback of Notre-Dame. Central figure of Romanticism.
64: English scientist who made major contributions to the fields of electromagnetism and electrochemistry.
65: Scottish physicist who formulated the equations of electromagnetism. Einstein called him the greatest physicist of the 19th century.
66: German theoretical physicist who developed the theory of relativity. Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921.
67: Serbian electrical inventor and engineer. He developed the alternating current system and contributed to the electromagnetic field.
68: Polish-French physicist and chemist. The first person to win two Nobel Prizes in different sciences.
69: Austrian neurologist and founder of psychoanalysis. His theories about the unconscious revolutionized psychology.
70: German physicist who originated quantum theory. Nobel Prize in Physics in 1918.
71: Danish physicist who made fundamental contributions to understanding atomic structure and quantum mechanics.
72: German physicist, pioneer of quantum mechanics. He formulated the uncertainty principle.
73: British politician and statesman. Prime Minister of the United Kingdom during World War II. Nobel Prize in Literature.
74: Indian political and spiritual leader. He led India's independence from British rule through nonviolent civil disobedience.
75: 32nd president of the United States. He led the country through the Great Depression and World War II.
76: Spanish painter and sculptor, co-founder of Cubism. One of the most influential figures in 20th-century art.
77: Czech German-language writer. His works The Metamorphosis and The Trial are pillars of modern literature.
78: American novelist and journalist. Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954. Author of The Old Man and the Sea.
79: American writer of the Jazz Age. Author of The Great Gatsby, a symbol of the 1920s.
80: British mathematician, logician, and cryptographer. Considered the father of computer science and artificial intelligence.
81: American Baptist minister and political activist. Leader of the African-American civil rights movement.
82: 35th president of the United States. He led the country during the Cuban Missile Crisis.
83: South African anti-apartheid leader and statesman. First Black president of South Africa. Nobel Peace Prize in 1993.
84: Physician, writer, and Marxist-Leninist politician, key figure of the Cuban Revolution.
85: American theoretical physicist and scientific director of the Manhattan Project, which developed the first atomic bomb.
86: Scottish physician and bacteriologist. He discovered penicillin in 1928, revolutionizing modern medicine.
87: Russian revolutionary politician and theorist. Leader of the Russian Revolution of 1917 and first head of state of the USSR.
88: Spanish Surrealist painter, known for his dreamlike imagery and precise painting technique.
89: Swiss psychiatrist and psychologist. Founder of analytical psychology. Student and later rival of Freud.
90: Greek philosopher and mathematician. Famous for the theorem bearing his name and for founding the Pythagorean school.
91: Greek historian and geographer. Considered the father of history for his work Histories on the Persian Wars.
92: Greek physician, considered the father of Western medicine. Author of the Hippocratic Oath.
93: Athenian historian and military commander. Author of the History of the Peloponnesian War, considered the first scientific historian.
94: Greek philosopher, principal representative of the Cynic school. Known for living in a barrel and challenging social conventions.
95: Greek philosopher and founder of Epicureanism. He advocated the pursuit of rational pleasure as the path to happiness.
96: Chinese general, military strategist, and philosopher. Author of The Art of War, the most influential military treatise in history.
97: Chinese philosopher, founder of Taoism. Author of the Tao Te Ching, one of the most translated texts in the world.
98: First-century Jewish preacher, central figure of Christianity. His life and teachings marked the beginning of the common era.
99: Arab prophet, founder of Islam. He unified the Arabian Peninsula under one religion and established the Quran.
100: Roman emperor. He legalized Christianity and founded Constantinople. His reign marked a key transition in history.
